seoTitle: Match Multiple Payments to One Invoice in QuickBooks
metaDescription: Learn how to record and link several partial payments to a single invoice in QuickBooks Online without leaving open balances.
intro: When a client pays in installments instead of one lump sum, keeping the invoice balance accurate takes a bit of extra care. This guide shows how to apply multiple partial payments to a single invoice in QuickBooks Online and match them to your bank feed.

steps.heading: Open Receive Payment for the first installment
steps.body: Click + New, choose Receive Payment, and select the customer to see their open invoices.

steps.heading: Apply the first partial amount
steps.body: Enter the value of the first installment in Amount Received and check the box next to the target invoice.

steps.heading: Save and repeat for the next payment
steps.body: Save the entry, then repeat the process for the second installment, applying it to the same invoice's remaining balance.

steps.heading: Confirm the invoice is fully covered
steps.body: Review the updated balance to make sure the combined payments equal the original amount due.

steps.heading: Match to your bank feed
steps.body: Go to Bank Transactions and click Match on each downloaded bank line to lock the statement rows against your recorded payments.

faq.question: Can I apply two separate bank deposits to one invoice?
faq.answer: Yes, you can record each payment separately through Receive Payment and apply both to the same invoice until it's fully paid.

faq.question: What happens to the invoice balance after a partial payment?
faq.answer: QuickBooks automatically reduces the invoice's outstanding balance by the amount applied.

faq.question: How do I know the invoice is fully settled?
faq.answer: Check the customer's ledger or invoice status to confirm it shows as fully paid once all installments are recorded.

faq.question: Can I match multiple bank feed lines to payments I already recorded?
faq.answer: Yes, use the Bank Transactions dashboard to match each incoming bank line to its corresponding recorded payment.
